query: clinical features of cingulate gyrus herniation in elderly patients with hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- History and Physical -- Clinical features of herniation
retrieved_docs.content: Subfalcine (cingulate) herniation: involves the cingulate gyrus and pericallosal arteries, producing leg weakness Lateral (uncal) herniation affects several structures, including: Oculomotor nerve causing ptosis and ipsilateral mydriasis Cerebral peduncle causing contralateral hemiparesis Posterior cerebral artery leading to decreased consciousness Posterior (tectal) herniation compresses the superior colliculi, resulting in bilateral ptosis and upward gaze paralysis Central (axial) herniation involves Perforating branches of the basilar artery resulting in depressed consciousness and impaired eye movement Midbrain, pons, and medulla causing respiratory irregularity or apnea Reticular formation causing hypertension and bradycardia Tonsillar herniation compresses the medulla, resulting in apnea

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, individual patient management may vary based on specific clinical circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Neuroimaging, such as brain CT or MRI, is crucial for confirming the diagnosis of hypertensive encephalopathy and ruling out other potential causes of neurological symptoms. CT is not sensitive enough to detect brain edema but is essential in ruling out other causes of altered mental status.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The most important points to remember in diagnosing and managing hypertensive encephalopathy are the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Putamen -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The putamen, as a common structure affected by a hypertensive cerebral hemorrhage, elicits a large range of presentations based on the magnitude of the initial blood extravasation. [24] Despite the controversy, surgical evacuation of the intracerebral hemorrhage is a mainstay therapeutic approach to decompress the mass effect and also eliminate the cytotoxic edema resulting from the ischemia and the degraded blood products. [25] Current recommendations to remove putaminal bleed include large size hematoma with life-threatening herniation, especially in young aged patients. [26] The other indication of removal of hematoma includes the presence of hemiparesis owing to the compression of the internal capsule from the hematoma that can be confirmed by the application of the MR tractography study (see Image. Coronal Section of Brain Through Anterior Commissure). [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cingulate Cortex -- Clinical Significance -- Subfalcine Herniation
retrieved_docs.content: Owing to the anatomical organization of the brain within a fixed compartment (the bony cranium) and subdivided by dural reflections (the falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli), it is vulnerable to damage from an increase in intracranial pressure (ICP). [21] This may be caused by an increased amount of intracranial cerebrospinal fluid (i.e., hydrocephalus), hematoma in the extradural, subdural, subarachnoid, or intraparenchymal spaces, presence of a space-occupying lesion, or edema from a disease process. [22] Following an increase in ICP, the brain can herniate between the spaces bound by the dural reflections, causing corresponding neurological damage to the structures involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cingulate Cortex -- Clinical Significance -- Subfalcine Herniation
retrieved_docs.content: The most common brain herniation syndrome is subfalcine herniation, in which raised ICP forces a cerebral hemisphere to the opposite side of the cranium, forcing the cingulate gyrus beneath the free edge of the falx cerebri. [23] This happens anteriorly because the falx cerebri is rigid posteriorly, so the herniation will occur preferentially anteriorly, where there is less resistance from the falx cerbri (see Image. Compression of the Pericallosal Artery). [24] Although it does not usually produce severe symptomatology, if subfalcine herniation progresses, it can be further complicated by compression of the anterior cerebral artery (ACA) against the falx, resulting in an ipsilateral ACA stroke which most commonly manifests as contralateral leg weakness. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Crisis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A CT scan of the brain is indicated if stroke or encephalopathy is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Herniation -- Pathophysiology -- 1. Subfalcine Herniation
retrieved_docs.content: In subfalcine herniation, the ipsilateral cingulate gyrus gets migrated beneath the anterior falx, resulting in infarction along with the distal territory of the anterior cerebral artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cortical dementias [34] : Alzheimer disease Frontotemporal dementia. Subcortical dementias: Lewy-body dementia Parkinson’s disease and vascular parkinsonism Progressive supranuclear palsy Corticobasal degeneration AIDS dementia complex Age-related depression Mixed dementias Vascular dementia

retrieved_docs.title: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Peri-ventricular hypodensities in CT or T2/fluid-attenuated inversion recovery hyperintensities in MRI: Represents transependymal edema due to elevated CSF pressure, but may also be seen in small vessel ischemic disease. There is bulging of the lateral ventricular roof (upward bowing and stretching of the corpus callosum). CSF flow study: the flow rate of greater than 24.5 mL/min is 95% specific for NPH. Aqueductal flow void seen in T2 weighted images due to increased CSF flow velocity is not a useful sign. Factors suggesting favorable outcome following shunt surgery: Aqueductal stroke volume greater than 42 microliters Lack of white matter lesions on MRI B-waves longer than 50% of intracranial pressure monitoring time Resistance to CSF outflow over 18 mmHg Factors suggesting unfavorable outcome following shunt surgery: Severe dementia Dementia as a presenting symptom MRI abnormalities, cerebral atrophy, and multiple white matter lesions Misdiagnosis and delayed recognition

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The internal carotid arteries originate in the neck from the common carotid arteries, ascend to the skull base, and enter the middle cranial fossa through the carotid canals. The carotid arteries pass anteromedially toward the cavernous sinus on the sphenoid body. The anterior and middle cerebral arteries are the internal carotid arteries' terminal branches, comprising the brain's anterior circulation. The anterior communicating artery bridges the right and left anterior cerebral arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Evaluation -- Imaging Studies
retrieved_docs.content: T2-weighted MRI can localize cerebral edema regions that may arise from hypertensive encephalopathy. PRES appears as bilateral white matter edema in the posterior cerebral hemispheres (see Image . Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome on Magnetic Resonance Imaging). Hypertensive brainstem encephalopathy may feature punctuate or confluent areas of increased signal. [14] The distribution of cerebral edema is localized, occurring in the parietooccipital regions in PRES and pontine region in cases of hypertensive brainstem encephalopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Increased Intracranial Pressure -- History and Physical -- Clinical features of herniation
retrieved_docs.content: Cushing triad is a clinical syndrome consisting of hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respiration, and is a sign of impending brain herniation. This occurs when the ICP is significantly elevated, and the elevation of blood pressure is a reflex mechanism to maintain CPP. High blood pressure can cause reflex bradycardia and compromise of the brain stem, which in turn affects respiration. Ultimately, the contents of the cranium are displaced downwards due to the high ICP, causing a phenomenon known as herniation, which can be potentially fatal. [6] The different types of herniation include:

retrieved_docs.title: Anton Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Recovery of visual function has occurred in conditions causing ABS, such as hypertensive encephalopathy and cortical hypoperfusion. In these conditions, correction of the causative factor may lead to the resolution of symptoms. The outcome of cortical blindness depends on the age of the patient, etiology, severity, duration, initial recovery time, medical history.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Encephalopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertensive encephalopathy symptoms can mimic other neurological emergencies such as cardiovascular accidents and seizures. Clinical assessment and neuroimaging are vital for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is clinically associated with several conditions and findings. Patients with essential hypertension often exhibit depressive disorders, may have abnormal chest X‑ray results, and can experience tremor. In addition, essential hypertension corresponds to both diastolic hypertension and systolic hypertension, reflecting its classification into the two primary components of elevated blood pressure. Recognizing these associations and the dual nature of essential hypertension aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2894
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral edema (تكثر الدبق الدماغي) may be a finding of disease that presents with headache (صداع), indicating that increased intracranial pressure or brain swelling can manifest as head pain. In addition, lumbar puncture (البزل القطني (LP)) is clinically associated with headache, reflecting the common post‑procedural symptom that patients may experience after cerebrospinal fluid sampling. Together, these connections underscore that headache can arise both from underlying intracranial pathology such as cerebral edema and from iatrogenic causes like lumbar puncture, highlighting the need for careful assessment of headache etiology in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: sites (brain, CSF pathways, nerves), overlapping clinical entities (dementia, gait disorder, vascular headache), and common therapeutic strategies (extracranial ventricular shunting, neurosurgery, valproic acid), emphasizing the need for coordinated assessment and management of overlapping neurological disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure is classified as a vascular condition that reflects an overall increase in arterial pressure. Within the broader vascular category, several specific hypertensive disorders are recognized as subtypes, including أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension). Although these entities share the common feature of elevated blood pressure, they differ in their underlying mechanisms, affected vascular territories, and clinical manifestations. Recognizing that elevated mean arterial pressure and its vascular subtypes are interrelated helps clinicians accurately diagnose, monitor, and tailor treatment strategies for patients presenting with various forms of hypertension, thereby improving patient care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: excess blood pressure), highlighting hypertensive encephalopathy as another related condition. “Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten” itself is positioned under the parent term “other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease,” reinforcing its status as a collection of poorly defined or miscellaneous cerebrovascular pathologies. Collectively, these relationships depict a structured network in which stroke serves as a central clinical event linked to various vascular abnormalities—such as cerebral artery sclerosis, arterial dissection, and hypertensive brain injury—each situated within broader taxonomic groups that span circulatory, psychosomatic, and ill‑defined cerebrovascular domains. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in categorizing cerebrovascular disorders, recognizing overlapping risk factors, and guiding comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: Emotional blunting (تبلد المشاعر) is identified as a clinical manifestation of specific brain atrophy (ضمور المخ المحدد). This relationship indicates that patients who exhibit localized cerebral atrophy may present with reduced emotional responsiveness, and recognizing emotional blunting as a sign of such atrophy can aid clinicians in diagnosing underlying neurological conditions where affective changes are a prominent feature.

Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1567
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified as a ventriculoatrial shunt procedure, reflecting its function of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to the atrium for the management of hydrocephalus and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 887
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) manifests as elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated systolic blood pressure, and elevated mean arterial pressure. It is linked to a hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) and to primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). Essential hypertension also maps to diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). Clinically, essential hypertension is associated with depressive disorders (اضطرابات اكتئابية), abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية), and tremor (رعاش). The vascular system (Vascular) is the hierarchical category that includes elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, diastolic hypertension, and systolic hypertension as its children. This integrated view highlights how essential hypertension presents with multiple blood‑pressure elevations, relates to other hypertensive subtypes, and co‑occurs with neuropsychiatric and respiratory signs, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and management within the vascular domain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: System” is identified as the common substrate for other and ill‑defined cerebrovascular disease (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten), renal vascular disorder, and arterial dissection, and serves as the parent for the specific brain injury caused by excess blood pressure (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم) within the cerebrovascular disease hierarchy. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how essential hypertension integrates with renal, cerebral, and systemic vascular pathologies, informing comprehensive clinical assessment and integrated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2757
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a primary form of high blood pressure that frequently presents with an elevated mean arterial pressure as a clinical manifestation. Hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) is closely linked to essential hypertension, indicating that severe acute blood‑pressure elevations can arise in patients with this condition. Essential hypertension also has a recognized relationship with metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض), reflecting shared pathophysiological mechanisms and overlapping risk factors. Moreover, essential hypertension is commonly mapped to systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقبالي), emphasizing that the systolic component often predominates in its presentation. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians identify the characteristic signs, potential complications, and associated comorbidities of essential hypertension, thereby improving patient assessment and management.
